senses.definition: to put effort into doing something unproductive and completely unnecessary

examples.x: Introducing a new fashion trend to a city that is already known for its fashion sense is like carrying owls to Athens - it's unnecessary and redundant.
Offering to teach someone how to use a computer program they are already skilled in is like bringing owls to Athens - it's unnecessary and redundant.
Trying to impress a group of musicians with your musical talent is like bringing owls to Athens - they already have plenty of talented musicians.

senses.definition: to undertake something that is pointless

examples.x: Introducing a new dance move to a group of professional dancers is like carrying coals to Newcastle - they already have plenty of moves.
Trying to sell umbrellas on a rainy day in a place where there are many umbrella vendors is like taking coals to Newcastle - it's unnecessary and redundant.

senses.definition: to pursue a goal or aspiration that is unrealistic or unlikely to be achieved

examples.x: He spent years chasing rainbows, pursuing a career in acting despite having little talent and few opportunities.
Trying to win the lottery is like chasing rainbows - the odds of winning are so low that it's not worth the effort.

senses.definition: to completely fail to produce the intended or expected effect

examples.x: Despite his best efforts, his attempts to reconcile with his estranged friend were for naught.
The company's investment in a new product line went for naught when it failed to generate significant revenue.
The team's hard work and dedication went for naught when they lost the championship game.

senses.definition: to fail to achieve the desired results

examples.x: Despite their best efforts, the negotiations with the other company came to nothing.
The promising business venture came to nothing when the economy took a downturn.

senses.definition: used to refer to something, such as a chance, idea, etc., that has been completely wasted or neglected

examples.x: The money spent on the renovation project was all down the drain when the contractor didn't finish the work.
The time spent waiting in traffic was all down the drain when he missed the important meeting.

senses.definition: to fail to produce or achieve the desired result

examples.x: His proposal for a new project fell on stony ground when no one was interested in investing.
The artist's new album fell on stony ground when it received negative reviews from critics and fans alike.

senses.definition: to fail to achieve success despite the attempts made

examples.x: All nothing but futile attempts that went nowhere.
He'll get nowhere with her, he's too young.
I'm trying to persuade her to come, but I'm getting nowhere.

senses.definition: with no success achieved or no progress made
to engage in a fruitless or pointless activity

examples.x: If we keep focusing on this issue, we'll just be milking the bull - it's time to move on to something else.
The politician's attempts to win over the opposing party were like trying to milk a bull - they were never going to work.
The project team is milking the ram by focusing on minor details instead of addressing the major issues.
The sales team was milking the ram by continuing to promote a product that was clearly not selling.
Trying to convince him to change his mind is like trying to milk a bull - it's just not going to happen.

word_list.pronunciation: pˈeɪ tˈuː dˈɪɹli fɔːɹ wˈʌnz wˈɪsəl

senses.definition: to waste too much money, resources, or effort on doing something that proves to be unfulfilling in the end

examples.x: If you don't take the time to read the contract carefully, you could end up paying too dearly for your whistle.
If you invest in that company without doing your research, you might pay too dearly for your whistle.
The athlete is risking injury by pushing themselves too hard in training - they may end up paying too dearly for their whistle.
The entrepreneur paid too dearly for their whistle by investing all their money in a business that ultimately failed.

senses.definition: a pursuit or search for something that is unlikely to produce any useful result

examples.x: Searching for the lost item without any leads is like going on a wild goose chase - it's unlikely that we'll find it.
The detective is chasing down a lead that is likely to be a wild goose chase, as it's based on unreliable information.
The team spent weeks on a wild goose chase trying to find a solution to the problem, only to realize that it was caused by a simple error.
The detective spent months following a lead that turned out to be a wild goose chase, leading him nowhere closer to solving the case.
Sarah's attempt to find her missing keys turned into a wild goose chase as she searched every corner of the house without any luck.

senses.definition: to come to realize what could have been done to prevent something bad from happening after it has already happened

examples.x: It's easy to be wise after the event, but we need to make the best decision we can with the information we have now.
Looking back, we can see that we were all wise after the event - there were warning signs that we should have paid attention to.
The team is being criticized for their decision, but it's important to remember that we're all wise after the event.
We may not know the right decision now, but we'll be wise after the event and learn from our mistakes.

senses.definition: to make an attempt to achieve something that seems extremely difficult or even impossible

examples.x: If we don't prioritize our goals and resources, we'll end up boiling the ocean instead of achieving meaningful results.
The company wasted months trying to develop a product that was too complex and ultimately failed - they were boiling the ocean.
The team is spending too much time on minor details instead of focusing on the bigger picture - they're boiling the ocean.
Trying to achieve perfection in every aspect of the project is like trying to boil the ocean - it's not a realistic goal.

senses.definition: to engage in a futile or meaningless action in an attempt to resolve a problem or accomplish a task

examples.x: I'm not sure what else I can do, I've tried everything, but it's like waving a dead chicken.
I'm starting to think that waving a dead chicken is the only way to get their attention.
I've been trying to get her to listen to me, but it's like waving a dead chicken.

word_list.pronunciation: pˌʊt ɐ lˈɪpstɪk ˌɑːn ɐ pˈɪɡ

senses.definition: to make a fruitless attempt in order to make something look more beautiful or successful

examples.x: The company is trying to improve their public image through a new advertising campaign, but it's just putting a lipstick on a pig - they need to address the underlying problems.
The politician tried to make their flawed policy sound better with clever phrasing, but it was just putting lipstick on a pig - the policy was still flawed.
The team was trying to salvage a failing project by making superficial changes, but it was just putting a lipstick on a pig - the project was still doomed to fail.
Trying to make a poorly designed website look better with a new color scheme is like putting lipstick on a pig - it won't fix the underlying issues.

senses.definition: to make a fruitless attempt to solve a problem

examples.x: If we don't have a solid plan in place, our efforts will be like whistling in the wind - we won't achieve anything meaningful.
The company's attempts to enter the market without doing proper research were like whistling in the wind - they were bound to fail.
The protestors are shouting slogans, but it's like whistling in the wind - the government is unlikely to respond to their demands.
Trying to convince the stubborn client to change their mind is like whistling in the wind - it's unlikely to have any effect.

senses.definition: to disagree with or continue arguing about a decision that is already made

examples.x: If we get bogged down arguing the toss over minor details, we'll never be able to accomplish anything meaningful.
The negotiation process was bogged down by the two sides arguing the toss over minor details, rather than focusing on the bigger picture.
The team was arguing the toss over the color scheme of the website, even though it was a minor issue that wouldn't affect its functionality.
The two colleagues are still arguing about who should take credit for the project, but it's just arguing the toss at this point.
